sources.notes: Read the range-query statement and constraints: total n and q are each at most 2e5, and equal ranges require output 0 for an unbounded modulus.
The official English editorial revision and reference implementation confirm adjacent absolute differences plus a GCD sparse table; the prose widget itself was dynamically unavailable.
The Luogu solution independently proves the difference-GCD characterization and supplies a segment-tree range-GCD implementation.

Proved both directions of the congruence/divisibility equivalence and exhaustively compared the formula with direct modulus enumeration on random arrays of length up to 7 over all intervals; all cases matched.

annotation.summary: For every subarray [l,r], find the largest modulus m for which all values have the same remainder, outputting 0 when every value is equal and m is unbounded.
annotation.constraints: Across test cases, sum n <= 2e5 and sum q <= 2e5, with a_i <= 1e9; each query therefore needs logarithmic or constant time after preprocessing.
annotation.core_idea: Values in a range are all congruent modulo m iff m divides every pairwise difference, and it is enough to require divisibility of adjacent differences. Therefore the maximum finite m is gcd(|a_l-a_{l+1}|,...,|a_{r-1}-a_r|). Build the adjacent-difference array and answer static range-GCD queries; gcd 0 exactly represents the all-equal, unbounded case required to print 0.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n + q) time and O(n log n) memory with a sparse table; a segment tree gives O((n+q) log n) time and O(n) memory.
tricks: Convert equal-remainder constraints into divisibility of differences.
Adjacent differences generate every pairwise difference by telescoping.
Use gcd 0 as the required sentinel for an all-equal interval.

solution_variants.name: difference-gcd-sparse-table
solution_variants.summary: Build absolute adjacent differences, preprocess an idempotent GCD sparse table, and query the difference interval [l,r-1] in O(1).
solution_variants.complexity: O(n log n + q) time and O(n log n) memory

solution_variants.name: difference-gcd-segment-tree
solution_variants.summary: Use the same adjacent-difference reduction but maintain range GCD with a segment tree, as in the consulted Luogu solution.
solution_variants.complexity: O((n+q) log n) time and O(n) memory

tags.evidence: The answer is exactly the GCD of all adjacent differences in the queried range; this equivalence is the mathematical core.
Replacing a by adjacent absolute differences turns a modulo-equality condition on [l,r] into one range aggregate query on [l,r-1].
The official main solution preprocesses static range GCD and answers each query in O(1) using overlapping sparse-table blocks.
A segment tree is a fully valid alternative range-GCD engine with O(log n) per query, independently confirmed by the Luogu solution.
